Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simile-based verb phrase with comparative particle 'like'
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Pretending to be ignorant or unaware to avoid responsibility or confrontation
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from local seafood imagery; the squid (sotong) metaphor humorously implies confusion or deliberate cluelessness in Singaporean slang
First Example Sentence: Eh don't act blur like sotong lah, you know the deadline already.
Second Example Sentence: Every meeting he act blur like sotong so people don't ask him do extra work.
Third Example Sentence: She saw the mistake but still act blur like sotong during discussion.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Intensified adjective phrase using Hokkien loanword 'kiasu'
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Extremely afraid of losing out or missing opportunities
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain Rephrasi: Very competitive or overly afraid of losing
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From Hokkien 'kia su' meaning fear of losing; widely associated with Singapore culture
First Example Sentence: That guy queue two hours for free tote bag, really kiasu until cannot.
Second Example Sentence: My colleague kiasu until cannot, every training also must sign up first.
Third Example Sentence: She submit the assignment one week early, kiasu until cannot.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Expression indicating approval, reliability, or impressive ability
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Influenced by English adjective 'steady' but reinterpreted in Singlish with pragmatic particle
First Example Sentence: You help me fix the computer so fast, steady lah bro.
Second Example Sentence: His presentation today really steady lah, everyone clap.
Third Example Sentence: The new intern solve the bug in ten minutes, steady lah.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Boasting or making grand claims without evidence
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reduplication common in Singlish for emphasis, reflecting Malay and Chinese linguistic influence
First Example Sentence: He always talk big big but the project still not done.
Second Example Sentence: During meeting she talk big big about strategy but never execute.
Third Example Sentence: Don't talk big big first, later cannot deliver very paiseh.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Single lexical borrowing functioning as adjective or noun
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Being overly curious or interfering in others' matters
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Borrowed from Hokkien 'kay poh', widely used across Singapore
First Example Sentence: Why you so kaypoh about their argument yesterday?
Second Example Sentence: My auntie downstairs very kaypoh, every day ask people question.
Third Example Sentence: Don't kaypoh lah, that issue not our business.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative modal verb phrase with Malay borrowing 'tahan'
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Unable to endure something unpleasant any longer
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay word 'tahan' meaning endure integrated into Singlish grammar
First Example Sentence: The heat today really cannot tahan.
Second Example Sentence: He keep complaining until everyone cannot tahan already.
Third Example Sentence: This queue so long, I really cannot tahan waiting.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Being assigned or forced to take responsibility unwillingly
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Singapore office slang referring to someone being metaphorically 'shot' with responsibility
First Example Sentence: During the meeting I kena arrowed to organize the event.
Second Example Sentence: The manager arrowed him to handle the difficult client.
Third Example Sentence: Somehow I always get arrowed to write the report.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Complaint that someone was not invited
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Hokkien phrase meaning 'didn't call/invite', popularized in Singapore youth slang
First Example Sentence: You all go makan without me, bo jio ah?
Second Example Sentence: They watch movie yesterday but bo jio me.
Third Example Sentence: Next time got gathering don't bo jio ok.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To deliberately cause trouble or embarrassment for someone
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Local clipped borrowing from 'sabotage' used humorously in Singapore English
First Example Sentence: You sabo me during presentation by asking that question.
Second Example Sentence: My friend sabo me by telling everyone my old nickname.
Third Example Sentence: Don't sabo your teammate like that lah.

Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Feeling extremely satisfied, pleased, or delighted
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay origin word meaning pleasure or deliciousness widely adopted in Singlish
First Example Sentence: This durian really shiok, creamy and sweet.
Second Example Sentence: After long run drink cold water damn shiok.
